banner: Week 6 · Prove It — Parallel & Perpendicular Lines; Bisectors
essential_question: Why does swinging two matched compass arcs always land exactly on a segment's midpoint — not just close to it?
objective: Justify why the perpendicular-bisector and angle-bisector constructions land exactly on their targets, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on parallel/perpendicular lines, bisectors, and constructions.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch a pair of parallel lines and a pair of perpendicular lines, marking the right angle on the perpendicular pair. Then, from memory, define bisector, perpendicular bisector, and midpoint in one sentence each.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 5A & 5B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 5A/5B. We check the tricky ones: the fill-in-the-blank definitions, and the two straightedge-and-compass items (finding a midpoint and its perpendicular bisector, and bisecting an angle). Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: the two points where your equal-radius arcs cross are each exactly the same distance from A as they are from B. Argue it — what does swinging an arc of one fixed radius from a point actually guarantee about every point on that arc?

Claim 2: not every bisector of segment AB is a perpendicular bisector — only ONE specific line through the midpoint earns that name. Argue why, and say what makes that one line special among all the others through the same midpoint.
Claim 3: in the angle-bisector construction, the two small halves ∠XYW and ∠WYZ are always exactly equal. Argue it from the two congruent triangles the equal-radius arcs create.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 6 — Supplementary, Complementary & Vertical Angles — where two intersecting lines start creating angle pairs with special relationships.

success_criteria.text: I can tell parallel and perpendicular lines apart and define bisector, perpendicular bisector, and midpoint without help
I can justify why the compass construction lands exactly on the midpoint and crosses at a right angle
I can construct and verify a perpendicular bisector and an angle bisector with a compass and straightedge

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W06 Parallel, Perpendicular & Bisectors.md

questions.prompt: Two lines in the same plane that never intersect are called…
questions.explain: Parallel lines lie in the same plane and stay the same distance apart forever — they never cross.

questions.prompt: A line, ray, or segment that cuts an angle or a segment into two congruent parts is called a…
questions.explain: A bisector's whole job is cutting an angle or a segment into two congruent (equal) parts.

questions.prompt: A bisector of a segment that also crosses it at a right angle is called a…
questions.explain: Adding the 90°-crossing condition to an ordinary bisector is exactly what makes it a perpendicular bisector.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why the two points where your equal-radius arcs cross are both exactly the same distance from A as they are from B — and why that guarantees the line through them passes through segment AB's true midpoint.
questions.answer: Each crossing point sits on an arc of the same radius drawn from A and an arc of the same radius drawn from B, so it is exactly that radius-distance from both A and B — it is equidistant from A and B. Any point equidistant from both endpoints must lie on the line that splits AB symmetrically, which crosses AB at its exact midpoint and at a right angle.
questions.explain: Full credit names 'equidistant from A and B' (same radius from each) as the reason the connecting line must pass through the true midpoint, not an estimate.

questions.prompt: On the grid, use your straightedge and compass to construct the perpendicular bisector of segment EF, and label its midpoint N.
questions.answer: Equal-radius arcs from E and from F crossing above and below the segment; a straight line through both crossings, labeled N where it meets EF.
questions.explain: Rubric: visible equal-radius arcs from both E and F (1 pt) + a correct connecting line meeting EF at its labeled midpoint N (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Draw ∠PQR at any opening you choose (vertex Q), then use your straightedge and compass to construct its bisector. Label a point S on the new ray so it reads ray QS.
questions.answer: One arc from Q crossing both rays; equal-radius arcs from those two crossing points meeting at a new point; ray QS drawn from the vertex through that point, splitting ∠PQR into two equal angles.
questions.explain: Rubric: visible arc from Q crossing both rays plus the two equal-radius interior arcs (1 pt) + ray QS correctly drawn through their crossing, bisecting the angle (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 5 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
